description: This figure displays the mechanical properties of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel treated with different cryogenic rolling (CR) thickness reductions (undeformed, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%). Subfigure (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for all samples: the undeformed specimen shows the highest elongation (~60%) and lowest peak stress (~882 MPa), while CR samples show progressively increased tensile strength and decreased failure strain with rising CR reduction. Subfigure (b) quantifies the evolution of four mechanical indicators as functions of CR deformation: ultimate tensile strength increases from 882 MPa to 1861 MPa, yield strength from 505 MPa to 1791 MPa, microhardness from 284 HV to 578 HV, while elongation drops from 64.5% to 6.6% as CR reduction rises to 90%. This figure demonstrates a clear strength-ductility trade-off of the steel during cryogenic rolling, where higher deformation enhances strength and hardness at the cost of ductility, driven by deformation-induced microstructural evolution including dislocation accumulation, grain refinement and deformation twinning.
